elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate under which circumstances there exists nonzero {\it{projective smooth $\field[G]$-modules, where $\field$ is a field of characteristic $p$ and $G$ is a locally pro-$p$ group.}}
elements.sent-2411-002: We prove the non-existence of (non-trivial) projective objects for so-called {\it{fair groups -- a family including $\bf{G (\frak{F )$ for a connected reductive group $\bf{G}$ defined over a non-archimedean local field $\frak{F}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-003: This was proved in \cite{SS24 for finite extensions $\frak{F /\Bbb{Q _p$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-004: The argument we present in this note has the benefit of being completely elementary and, perhaps more importantly, adaptable to $\frak{F =\Bbb{F _q(\!(t)\!)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-005: Finally, we elucidate the fairness condition via a criterion in the Chabauty space of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-006: Projective objects play a prominent role in the modular representation theory of a finite group $G$.
elements.sent-2411-007: For instance, if $\field$ is a field of characteristic $p$ dividing $|G|$, the Grothendieck group $K_0(\field[G])$ is part of the Cartan-Brauer triangle \cite[p.~46]{Sch13 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Also, in the very definition of the all-important stable module category of $\field[G]$ one kills morphisms which factor through a projective module.
elements.sent-2411-009: One side of the $p$-adic Langlands correspondence (or rather its mod $p$ counterpart) involves smooth $\field[G]$-modules where $G$ is now an infinite $p$-adic reductive group, and $\field$ is still a field of characteristic $p$.
elements.sent-2411-010: As was shown in \cite{SS24 this situation is dramatically different from the case of finite groups: There are no projective objects in $\Mod_{\field (G)$ other than $V=\{0\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: We mention in passing that the complex case is dissimilar: If $G$ is a $p$-adic reductive group, and we fix a character $\chi$ of the center, the category $\Mod_{\C ^\chi(G)$ of smooth $\C[G]$-modules with central character $\chi$ has lots of projective objects.}
elements.sent-2411-012: In fact, any irreducible supercuspidal representation is projective (and injective).
elements.sent-2411-013: See \cite{AR04 for more precise results in this direction -- including a converse.}
elements.sent-2411-014: One of the goals of this note is to extend parts of \cite{SS24 to groups $G=\bf{G (\frak{F )$ where $\bf{G}$ is a connected reductive group over any non-archimedean local field $\frak{F}$ -- possibly of positive characteristic.}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: The arguments in \cite{SS24 make heavy use of Poincar\'{e subgroups, and therefore only apply for finite extensions of $\Bbb{Q _p$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: We stress that \cite{SS24 had a different goal (to understand the derived functors of smooth induction) and the non-existence of nonzero projective objects was a byproduct.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The methods of this note are more elementary and completely avoid cohomology.
elements.sent-2411-019: We continue to let $\field$ denote a field of characteristic $p$, but we allow $G$ to be any locally pro-$p$ group (by which we mean it admits an open subgroup which is pro-$p$ in the induced topology).
elements.sent-2411-020: If $G$ is discrete, smoothness is automatic, and we obviously have plenty of projective $\field[G]$-modules -- such as $\field[G]$ itself.
elements.sent-2411-021: For that reason we will always assume $G$ is non-discrete.
elements.sent-2411-022: The key hypothesis is the following: We assume $G$ admits an open subgroup $K$ such that for all open subgroups $H \subset K$ there exists an open subgroup $H' \subsetneq H$ for which we have strict inclusions \begin{equation}\label{HYP} K \cap gH'g^{-1} \subsetneq K \cap gHg^{-1}\end{equation} for all $g \in G$.
elements.sent-2411-023: This condition also appeared in \cite{SS24 where it was shown to control the vanishing of the top derived functor $R^d\Ind_K^G(\field)$ for a $d$-dimensional $p$-adic Lie group $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-024: A pair $(G,K)$ satisfying (\ref{HYP ) is called fair in this note.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Non-trivial reductive groups $G=\bf{G (\frak{F )$ as above always admit a subgroup $K$ for which $(G,K)$ is fair.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: In fact one can take any compact open subgroup $K \subset G$, as follows easily from Bruhat-Tits theory.
elements.sent-2411-027: We remark that in general (\ref{HYP ) implies $G$ is non-discrete (take $H=\{e\}$).}
elements.sent-2411-028: With this terminology our main result is the following: \begin{thm Let $\field$ be a field of characteristic $p>0$.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Let $G$ be a locally pro-$p$ group which admits an open subgroup $K$ such that $(G,K)$ is fair, i.e. satisfies (\ref{HYP ).}
elements.sent-2411-030: Then the category of smooth $\field[G]$-modules $\Mod_{\field (G)$ has no nonzero projective objects.}
elements.sent-2411-031: In fact our Theorem \ref{cmain gives a stronger result than Theorem \ref{intromain : In Section \ref{cchar we consider the category of representations with a fixed central character.}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: More precisely, we fix a closed central subgroup $C \subset G$, a continuous character $\chi: C \rightarrow \field^\times$, and we show that the category $\Mod_{\field ^\chi(G)$ (of smooth $\field[G]$-modules on which $C$ acts via $\chi$) has no nonzero projective objects if $(G/C, KC/C)$ is fair ($\Rightarrow$ $C$ is not open).}
elements.sent-2411-033: For the sake of exposition we have emphasized the case where $C$ is trivial here in the introduction.
elements.sent-2411-034: Fixing the central character may seem like a nuance, but the categories appearing in the $p$-adic local Langlands program for $\GL_2(\Q_p)$ consist of representations with a fixed central character.

elements.sent-2411-195: One solution addressing the lack of projective objects in $\Mod_{\field (G)$ is to endow this category with other exact structures.}
elements.sent-2411-196: The survey \cite{Buh10 serves as our main reference for the basic notions and properties of exact categories.}
elements.sent-2411-197: Let $\EE_\text{max}$ be the class of all short exact sequences $0 \rightarrow V'\rightarrow V \rightarrow V''\rightarrow 0$ in $\Mod_{\field (G)$.}
elements.sent-2411-198: For a fixed open subgroup $U \subset G$ we consider the class $\EE_U \subset \EE_\text{max}$ of all such sequences which split in $\Mod_{\field (U)$.}
elements.sent-2411-199: The axioms in \cite[Df.~2.1]{Buh10 are easy to verify.}
elements.sent-2411-200: This is precisely \cite[Exc.~5.3]{Buh10 applied to the restriction functor $\Mod_{\field (G)\rightarrow \Mod_{\field (U)$ (with split exact sequences).}}}
elements.sent-2411-202: We use the notation $\rightarrowtail$ for admissible monics.
elements.sent-2411-203: Similarly, an admissible epic is a morphism $\beta: V \rightarrow V''$ in $\Mod_{\field (G)$ which admits a $U$-equivariant section (a morphism $\sigma: V'' \rightarrow V$ in $\Mod_{\field (U)$ such that $\beta \circ \sigma=\Id_{V''}$).}}
elements.sent-2411-204: We use the notation $\twoheadrightarrow$ for admissible epics.
elements.sent-2411-205: Projective and injective objects of $(\Mod_{\field (G),\EE_U)$ are defined in \cite[Df.~11.1]{Buh10 .}}
elements.sent-2411-206: For example, $P$ is projective if every admissible epic $\beta: V\twoheadrightarrow V''$ induces a surjective map $\beta_*: \Hom_{\Mod_{\field (G) (P,V)\rightarrow \Hom_{\Mod_{\field (G) (P,V'')$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-208: The exact category $(\Mod_{\field (G),\EE_U)$ has enough projectives (see \cite[Df.~11.9]{Buh10 ) and enough injectives.}}
elements.sent-2411-209: The projective objects are precisely the direct summands of representations of the form $\ind_U^G(W)$ with $W \in \Mod_{\field (U)$.}
elements.sent-2411-210: The injective objects are the summands of $\Ind_U^G(W)$ as $W$ varies.
elements.sent-2411-211: If $G$ is compact, $(\Mod_{\field (G),\EE_U)$ is a Frobenius category (see \cite[Sect.~13.4]{Buh10 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-212: For part (a) let $X$ be an arbitrary object of $\Mod_{\field (G)$ and consider the counit of adjunction \begin{equation}B: \ind_U^G(X|_U) \longrightarrow X f \longmapsto {\sum}_{g \in U \backslash G} g^{-1}f(g).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-214: Indeed $B$ has a $U$-equivariant section $x \mapsto f_x$, where $f_x$ denotes the function in $\ind_U^G(X|_U)$ supported on $U$ and sending the identity to $x$.
elements.sent-2411-215: Also, $\ind_U^G(X|_U)$ is projective in $(\Mod_{\field (G),\EE_U)$ for the following reason.}
elements.sent-2411-216: Any admissible epic $\beta: V\twoheadrightarrow V''$ as above induces a surjective map \begin{equation}\Hom_{\Mod_{\field}(U)}(X|_U,V) \longrightarrow \Hom_{\Mod_{\field}(U)}(X|_U,V'')\end{equation} since $\sigma \circ (-)$ is a right inverse.
elements.sent-2411-217: By Frobenius reciprocity this amounts to $\beta_*$ being surjective.
elements.sent-2411-218: We conclude that for any $X$ there is an admissible epic $P \twoheadrightarrow X$ with $P$ projective.
elements.sent-2411-219: To show $(\Mod_{\field (G),\EE_U)$ also has enough injectives we consider the unit of adjunction \begin{equation}A: X \longrightarrow \Ind_U^G(X|_U) x \longmapsto [g \mapsto gx].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-221: A $U$-equivariant retraction is given by evaluation at the identity.
elements.sent-2411-222: It remains to note that $\Ind_U^G(X|_U)$ is injective.
elements.sent-2411-223: So, let $\alpha: V' \rightarrowtail V$ be an admissible monic as above.
elements.sent-2411-224: Again, by Frobenius reciprocity for the full induction, it suffices to observe that pulling back via $\alpha$ induces a surjective map \begin{equation}\Hom_{\Mod_{\field}(U)}(V, X|_U)\longrightarrow \Hom_{\Mod_{\field}(U)}(V', X|_U)\end{equation} since $(-)\circ \rho$ is a left inverse.
elements.sent-2411-225: Hence any $X$ admits an admissible monic $X \rightarrowtail I$ into an injective $I$.
elements.sent-2411-226: In the previous proofs of the projectivity of $\ind_U^G(X|_U)$ and the injectivity of $\Ind_U^G(X|_U)$ there was nothing special about $X|_U$.
elements.sent-2411-227: We can run the exact same arguments for any $U$-representation $W$ instead of $X|_U$.

elements.sent-2411-230: Any Frobenius category has an associated stable category, which is triangulated.
elements.sent-2411-231: As described in \cite[Sect.~6]{Kel96 this construction can be mimicked in greater generality.}
elements.sent-2411-232: In our setup the injectively stable category $S_\text{in (G,U)$ has the same objects as $\Mod_{\field (G)$ but the morphisms are \begin{equation}\Hom_{S_in(G,U)}(V_1,V_2)=\Hom_{\Mod_{\field}(G)}(V_1,V_2)/I(V_1,V_2)\end{equation} where $\mathcal{I (V_1,V_2)$ is the space of morphisms $V_1\rightarrow V_2$ which factor through an injective object (with respect to the exact structure $\EE_U$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-233: The suspension functor $T: S_\text{in (G,U) \longrightarrow S_\text{in (G,U)$ has the property that there is a short exact sequence \begin{equation}0 \rightarrow X \overset{A}{\rightarrow} \Ind_U^G(X|_U) \rightarrow T(X) \rightarrow 0\end{equation} in $\EE_U$ for all $X$ (where $A$ is the adjunction map which appeared in the proof of Proposition \ref{injpr ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: In other words $T(X)$ is the cokernel of $A$.
elements.sent-2411-235: By \cite[Thm.~6.2]{Kel96 this gives $S_\text{in (G,U)$ the structure of a {\it{suspended category (see \cite[Sect.~7]{Kel96 ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-236: Essentially what this means is it satisfies all the axioms for a triangulated category except that the suspension functor need not be an equivalence.
elements.sent-2411-237: Similarly, the projectively stable category $S_\text{pr (G,U)$ is defined by modding out morphisms which factor through a projective object.}
elements.sent-2411-238: In this case there is a functor $\Omega: S_\text{pr (G,U) \longrightarrow S_\text{pr (G,U)$ such that there is an exact sequence \begin{equation}0 \rightarrow \Omega(X) \rightarrow \ind_U^G(X|_U) \overset{B}{\rightarrow} X \rightarrow 0\end{equation} in $\EE_U$ for all $X$ (with $B$ as in the proof of Proposition \ref{injpr ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-240: When $G$ is compact we have a Frobenius category.
elements.sent-2411-241: In this case $S_\text{in (G,U)=S_\text{pr (G,U)$ and $T$, $\Omega$ are mutually quasi-inverse equivalences of categories.}}
elements.sent-2411-243: When $G$ is finite and $U=\{e\}$ the above construction yields the stable module category $S(G)$ which is of pivotal importance in modular representation theory (when $|G|$ is divisible by the characteristic of $\field$).
elements.sent-2411-245: We are optimistic that $S_\text{in (G,U)$ and $S_\text{pr (G,U)$ will likewise play a central role in modular representation theory for non-compact groups, and we hope to explore this in continuation of this paper.}}
elements.section-2411-007: An interpretation of fairness in the Chabauty space
elements.sent-2411-246: The set $\mS(G)=\{\text{closed subgroups of $G$}\}$ carries a natural topology which makes it a compact Hausdorff space; see \cite{Cha50 .}
